title: Functions: domain, range, composite and inverse
summary: A function is the central object of the whole course, and this lesson builds the full 2.2 toolkit around it: function notation and what actually makes a rule a function (mapping, one-to-one versus many-to-one), the domain and range and how to find them, composite functions $(f\circ g)(x)=f(g(x))$ and why order matters, and inverse functions $f^{-1}$ â€” when they exist, how they reflect in the line $y=x$, how they swap domain and range, and how to find one algebraically. Because Analysis and Approaches marks Paper 1 line by line, every worked example is laid out exactly the way our marking engine awards the marks, so you can see where each method mark and accuracy mark is earned.
learningObjectives: Use function notation and decide whether a mapping is a function, distinguishing one-to-one from many-to-one.
Determine the domain and range of a function, including restrictions from division by zero and even roots.
Form composite functions $(f\circ g)(x)=f(g(x))$, evaluate them, and explain why order matters.
Find the inverse $f^{-1}$ of a one-to-one function algebraically, and state its domain and range by swapping those of $f$.
Recognise $y=f^{-1}(x)$ as the reflection of $y=f(x)$ in the line $y=x$, and present working so method (M) and accuracy (A) marks are secured.
levelNote: Common to SL and HL at this level.

simpleExplanation.title: Function Machines: Inputs, Outputs, and Reversing the Process
simpleExplanation.summary: A function is a machine: put an allowed input in (the domain) and exactly one output comes out (the range). You can feed one machine's output into another (composite functions), and if the machine never sends two inputs to the same output, you can build a machine that runs it backwards (the inverse).
simpleExplanation.analogy: Think of a recipe. The ingredients you are allowed to use are the domain, and the finished dishes you can make are the range. A composite function is using your finished cake as an ingredient in a trifle â€” the order matters, because a trifle made from cake is not the same as a cake made from trifle. An inverse function is looking at the finished trifle and working backwards to recover the exact original ingredients, which is only possible if no two ingredient lists ever produce the same trifle.
steps: Identify the allowed inputs (domain). Watch for the two no-go zones: never divide by zero, and never take an even root of a negative number.
Determine the possible outputs (range). Think about what the rule does â€” squaring is never negative, a reciprocal is never zero â€” and use minima, maxima and asymptotes.
For a composite $f(g(x))$, work from the inside out: substitute the whole expression for the inner function $g(x)$ into every $x$ of the outer function $f(x)$.
For an inverse, first check the function is one-to-one; then write $y=f(x)$, swap $x$ and $y$, and solve for $y$.

keyConcepts: inverse and reflection in y = x

flashcards.back: A rule (mapping) that assigns to every input in its domain exactly one output. If a single input could give two different outputs, it is not a function.
The set of all permitted input values ($x$-values) for which the function is defined.
The set of all output values ($f(x)$-values) the function actually produces as $x$ runs over the domain.

One-to-one: every output comes from exactly one input (passes the horizontal line test). Many-to-one: at least one output comes from two or more inputs, e.g. $f(x)=x^2$ where $x=2$ and $x=-2$ both give $4$.
All real $x$ except those making the denominator zero: exclude the solutions of $q(x)=0$.
The expression under an even root must be non-negative: solve $p(x)\ge 0$.
Apply $g$ first, then $f$: $(f\circ g)(x)=f(g(x))$. Substitute the whole of $g(x)$ into $f$.

flashcards.front: Is $f\circ g$ the same as $g\circ f$?
flashcards.back: Almost never. Composition is not commutative â€” the order of application changes the result. Always check which function is applied first.
Only when $f$ is one-to-one on its domain (horizontal line test). A many-to-one function must have its domain restricted before it can be inverted.
Write $y=f(x)$, swap $x$ and $y$, then solve for $y$. The result is $f^{-1}(x)$.
They swap: the domain of $f^{-1}$ is the range of $f$, and the range of $f^{-1}$ is the domain of $f$.
The reflection of $y=f(x)$ in the line $y=x$. Any point $(a,b)$ on $f$ becomes $(b,a)$ on $f^{-1}$.
Both simplify to $x$ (each on the appropriate domain). Composing a function with its inverse gives back the input â€” the two operations cancel.
$f^{-1}(x)$ is the inverse FUNCTION, not $\dfrac{1}{f(x)}$. For $f(x)=x+2$, the inverse is $f^{-1}(x)=x-2$, whereas the reciprocal is $\dfrac{1}{x+2}$ â€” completely different.

quickCheck.prompt: For $f(x)=2x$ and $g(x)=x+3$, which statement about $(f\circ g)(x)$ and $(g\circ f)(x)$ is correct?
options: $(f\circ g)(x)=2x+6$ and $(g\circ f)(x)=2x+3$, so they differ
They are equal because composition is commutative
quickCheck.answer: $(f\circ g)(x)=2x+6$ and $(g\circ f)(x)=2x+3$, so they differ

quickCheck.prompt: A function $f$ has domain $x\ge 0$ and range $y\ge 2$. The domain of $f^{-1}$ is:
Which function does NOT have an inverse over its entire (unrestricted) real domain?
For $f(x)=x^2+1$ and $g(x)=x-4$, the value of $(f\circ g)(6)$ is:

faq.q: How can I tell from a graph whether a function has an inverse?
faq.a: Use the horizontal line test. If any horizontal line meets the graph more than once, two different inputs share an output, the function is many-to-one, and it has no inverse over that domain. For example $y=x^2$ fails the test, but restricting its domain to $x\ge 0$ makes it one-to-one, and then the inverse is $y=\sqrt{x}$.

faq.q: What is the difference between $(f\circ g)(x)$ and $f(x)\,g(x)$?
faq.a: $(f\circ g)(x)=f(g(x))$ is composition â€” you substitute $g(x)$ into $f$. In contrast $f(x)\,g(x)$ is the product â€” you multiply the two expressions. They are different operations and almost always give different answers, so read the notation carefully.

The superscript $-1$ on a function name is reserved notation for the inverse function â€” the rule that undoes $f$ â€” not a power. The reciprocal $\dfrac{1}{f(x)}$ would be written $[f(x)]^{-1}$. For $f(x)=x+2$ the inverse is $f^{-1}(x)=x-2$, while the reciprocal is $\dfrac{1}{x+2}$; you can see at a glance they are unrelated.

faq.q: Why is the domain of a composite $f(g(x))$ sometimes restricted?
faq.a: There are two layers. First, $x$ must lie in the domain of the inner function $g$. Second, the output $g(x)$ must lie in the domain of the outer function $f$. For $f(x)=\sqrt{x}$ and $g(x)=x-5$, the composite $f(g(x))=\sqrt{x-5}$ needs $x-5\ge 0$, so the domain is $x\ge 5$.

faq.q: How do the graphs of $f$ and $f^{-1}$ relate, and why?
faq.a: The graph of $y=f^{-1}(x)$ is the reflection of $y=f(x)$ in the line $y=x$. This is because finding an inverse swaps the roles of input and output, so every point $(a,b)$ on $f$ corresponds to $(b,a)$ on $f^{-1}$, and reflecting in $y=x$ is exactly what swaps a point's coordinates.

sections.content: A function describes a relationship in which every allowed input produces exactly one output. It is the object almost every later topic is built on â€” from transformations and quadratics to calculus â€” so it repays being understood completely. In this lesson we pin down four things: what makes a rule a function and whether it is one-to-one, how to find the domain (allowed inputs) and range (possible outputs), how to combine functions into composites, and how to reverse a function to find its inverse. As in every Paper 1 topic, the worked examples are written exactly the way our engine marks your own practice: the method is on the page, mark by mark.

A function $f$ is a rule that maps each input in its domain to exactly one output. We write $f(x)$ for the output when the input is $x$, and $f:x\mapsto f(x)$ for the mapping itself. The single-output requirement is the whole point: a rule that could send one input to two different outputs is not a function. Functions are then classified by how outputs are shared. A function is one-to-one if every output comes from exactly one input, and many-to-one if some output is produced by two or more different inputs. This distinction decides later whether an inverse can exist.

items: **Function:** each input gives exactly one output. Graphically it passes the vertical line test â€” no vertical line meets the graph twice.
**One-to-one:** each output comes from exactly one input; the graph passes the horizontal line test. Example: $f(x)=3x-2$.
**Many-to-one:** at least one output comes from several inputs. Example: $f(x)=x^2$, where $x=2$ and $x=-2$ both map to $4$.
Only one-to-one functions can be inverted over their whole domain â€” this is why the classification matters.

sections.content: Domain and range: the function's boundaries

The domain is the complete set of permitted inputs; the range is the complete set of outputs the function actually produces. When finding a domain in SL Maths, watch for the two standard restrictions: you may never divide by zero, and you may never take an even root (such as a square root) of a negative number. Finding a range is about what the rule does to its inputs â€” squaring can never give a negative, a reciprocal can never give zero â€” so look for maxima, minima and asymptotes.

items: For $f(x)=\dfrac{p(x)}{q(x)}$, the domain excludes every $x$ with $q(x)=0$.
For $f(x)=\sqrt{p(x)}$, the domain requires $p(x)\ge 0$.
Find the range by tracking the outputs across the whole domain â€” use turning points, end behaviour and asymptotes.
Set notation is expected: e.g. $\{x\in\mathbb{R}\mid x\neq 2\}$ for a domain, $\{y\in\mathbb{R}\mid y\neq 0\}$ for a range.

sections.question: Consider $f(x)=\dfrac{5}{x-2}$. Find the domain and range of $f$. [4]
sections.solution:
**Domain â€” avoid division by zero.** The denominator is $x-2$.
Set it to zero: $x-2=0\implies x=2$.  [M1: identify the excluded value]
The function is defined for every real number except $x=2$.
Domain: $\{x\in\mathbb{R}\mid x\neq 2\}$.  [A1]

**Range â€” what outputs are possible.** A fraction equals zero only if its numerator is zero. Here the numerator is the constant $5$, so $y=\dfrac{5}{x-2}$ can never be $0$.  [M1: reason why $y=0$ is impossible]
Every other real value can be produced, so the range is $\{y\in\mathbb{R}\mid y\neq 0\}$.  [A1]

**Final answer:** domain $\{x\in\mathbb{R}\mid x\neq 2\}$, range $\{y\in\mathbb{R}\mid y\neq 0\}$.

sections.content: A composite function is formed when the output of one function becomes the input of another. The notation $(f\circ g)(x)$ reads 'f composed with g of x' and means: apply $g$ first, then feed its result into $f$. You build it by substituting the whole expression for $g(x)$ into every $x$ of $f(x)$, working from the inside out.

Order is everything. $(f\circ g)(x)$ applies $g$ first, then $f$; $(g\circ f)(x)$ applies $f$ first, then $g$. These almost always give different expressions, so before substituting, write down which function is the inner one. Getting the order backwards is one of the most common ways to lose the accuracy mark here.

sections.question: Let $f(x)=3x-4$ and $g(x)=x^2+1$. (a) Find an expression for $(f\circ g)(x)$. (b) Find the value of $(g\circ f)(2)$. [4]
sections.solution:
**(a) $(f\circ g)(x)$ â€” substitute $g$ into $f$.** Here $g$ is the inner function.
$(f\circ g)(x)=f(g(x))=f(x^2+1)$  [M1: substitute the whole of $g(x)$ into $f$]
$=3(x^2+1)-4 = 3x^2+3-4 = 3x^2-1$.  [A1]

**(b) $(g\circ f)(2)$ â€” inner function first.** Now $f$ is applied first.
$f(2)=3(2)-4=2$.  [M1: evaluate the inner function]
$g(f(2))=g(2)=(2)^2+1=5$.  [A1]

**Final answer:** (a) $(f\circ g)(x)=3x^2-1$; (b) $(g\circ f)(2)=5$.

sections.content: An inverse function $f^{-1}$ undoes the action of $f$: if $f$ maps $a$ to $b$, then $f^{-1}$ maps $b$ back to $a$. For an inverse to exist over the whole domain, $f$ must be one-to-one â€” otherwise a single output would have to map back to two different inputs, which no function is allowed to do. You can test this graphically with the horizontal line test. To find the inverse algebraically, write $y=f(x)$, swap $x$ and $y$, and solve for $y$; swapping the variables is precisely what reverses input and output.

items: **Existence:** $f^{-1}$ exists only if $f$ is one-to-one (horizontal line test). A many-to-one function needs its domain restricted first â€” e.g. restrict $f(x)=x^2$ to $x\ge 0$.
**Algebra:** write $y=f(x)$, swap $x$ and $y$, solve for $y$; then $y=f^{-1}(x)$.
**Domain and range swap:** domain of $f^{-1}$ = range of $f$; range of $f^{-1}$ = domain of $f$.
**Graph:** $y=f^{-1}(x)$ is the reflection of $y=f(x)$ in the line $y=x$; every point $(a,b)$ becomes $(b,a)$.
**Cancellation:** $f(f^{-1}(x))=x$ and $f^{-1}(f(x))=x$ â€” composing a function with its inverse returns the input.

sections.question: Let $g(x)=2x^3+5$. Find the inverse function $g^{-1}(x)$. [4]
sections.solution:
First note $g$ is one-to-one (a cubic through a single stretch and shift), so an inverse exists.
Let $y=g(x)$: $\quad y=2x^3+5$.
Swap $x$ and $y$:  [M1: correct method â€” swap the variables]
$x=2y^3+5$.
Solve for $y$:
$x-5=2y^3$  [A1: isolate the term in $y$]
$\dfrac{x-5}{2}=y^3$
$y=\sqrt[3]{\dfrac{x-5}{2}}$.  [A1]
Therefore $g^{-1}(x)=\sqrt[3]{\dfrac{x-5}{2}}$.  [A1: final answer, accept equivalent forms]

**Check (optional but reassuring):** $g(g^{-1}(x))=2\!\left(\sqrt[3]{\tfrac{x-5}{2}}\right)^{3}+5=2\cdot\tfrac{x-5}{2}+5=x.$

sections.content: The notation $f^{-1}(x)$ is standard IB notation for the inverse function. Never read it as the reciprocal $[f(x)]^{-1}=\dfrac{1}{f(x)}$. For $f(x)=x+2$, the inverse is $f^{-1}(x)=x-2$, but the reciprocal is $\dfrac{1}{x+2}$ â€” a completely different object. Mixing these up is a guaranteed way to lose marks.

items: **Reversing the order of composition** â€” $(f\circ g)(x)=f(g(x))$ applies $g$ first. Writing $g(f(x))$ instead loses the accuracy mark, because $f\circ g$ and $g\circ f$ are almost never equal.
**Reading $f^{-1}(x)$ as a reciprocal** â€” the inverse function is not $\dfrac{1}{f(x)}$. The $-1$ is inverse-function notation, not a power.
**Forgetting that the domain of $f^{-1}$ is the range of $f$** â€” you cannot just keep the original domain; the domain and range swap when you invert.
**Inverting a many-to-one function without restricting the domain** â€” $f(x)=x^2$ has no inverse until you restrict it (e.g. to $x\ge 0$); state the restriction.
**Ignoring domain restrictions on a composite** â€” the inner output must be a valid input for the outer function, so $\sqrt{x-5}$ needs $x\ge 5$, not all reals.
**Missing the exclusions when finding a domain** â€” forgetting to exclude values that make a denominator zero, or to require the expression under an even root to be $\ge 0$.
**Sloppy or missing set notation** â€” write domains and ranges properly, e.g. $\{x\in\mathbb{R}\mid x\neq 2\}$, rather than a vague phrase; examiners expect the notation.

sections.content: Model answer â€” marked the way our engine marks it

In Paper 1 the marks are analytic: each is tied to a specific line of working. A method mark (M) rewards a correct approach â€” a valid substitution or a sound rearrangement â€” even if the arithmetic later slips. An accuracy mark (A) rewards a correct result and is dependent on the corresponding method being present. IB conventions also let follow-through (FT) protect a later mark that is correctly worked from an earlier (possibly wrong) value, allow 'ignore subsequent working' (ISW) once a correct answer is seen, award an answer that is given (AG) only when the full derivation is shown, and accept any equivalent correct form. Study how each mark below is earned by one specific line.

sections.question: Given $f(x)=3x-2$ and $g(x)=x^2+1$, find $(f\circ g)(x)$ and $f^{-1}(x)$. [5]
sections.solution:
**Model answer â€” full working.**

**Part 1: the composite $(f\circ g)(x)$.** Apply $g$ first, then $f$, so substitute $g(x)=x^2+1$ into $f$.
$(f\circ g)(x)=f(g(x))=f(x^2+1)$
$=3(x^2+1)-2$
$=3x^2+3-2$
$=3x^2+1$.

**Part 2: the inverse $f^{-1}(x)$.** The function $f(x)=3x-2$ is linear, hence one-to-one, so an inverse exists.
Let $y=3x-2$.
Swap $x$ and $y$: $\quad x=3y-2$.
Solve for $y$: $\quad x+2=3y \implies y=\dfrac{x+2}{3}$.
Therefore $f^{-1}(x)=\dfrac{x+2}{3}$.

**Check:** $f(f^{-1}(x))=3\!\left(\dfrac{x+2}{3}\right)-2=(x+2)-2=x.$ âś“

**Final answer:** $(f\circ g)(x)=3x^2+1$ and $f^{-1}(x)=\dfrac{x+2}{3}$.

---
**How our marking engine awards the 5 marks:**

- **M1 â€” substitute $g$ into $f$.** Awarded for the correct method $f(g(x))=3(x^2+1)-2$: putting the whole of $g(x)$ into $f$ in the right order. This is a METHOD mark, earned for the approach even if the expansion later slips.
- **A1 â€” $(f\circ g)(x)=3x^2+1$.** The accuracy mark for the correct simplified composite. It depends on the M1 above; any equivalent form (e.g. $1+3x^2$) is accepted.
- **M1 â€” write $y=3x-2$ and swap the variables.** Awarded for the correct inverse method: setting $y=f(x)$ and interchanging $x$ and $y$ to get $x=3y-2$.
- **M1 â€” solve for $x$ (rearrange for the new $y$).** Awarded for correctly rearranging $x=3y-2$ towards $y$, i.e. $x+2=3y$. A second method mark, because isolating the variable is a distinct step.
- **A1 â€” $f^{-1}(x)=\dfrac{x+2}{3}$.** The final accuracy mark. FOLLOW-THROUGH (FT) applies: a candidate who made a slip in the composite but then carried out the inverse method correctly still earns the inverse marks on their own working. EQUIVALENT FORMS are accepted, so $\dfrac{1}{3}x+\dfrac{2}{3}$ scores full marks.

**Conventions in play.** Because A-marks are *dependent* on their M-mark, showing the substitution and the swap is what unlocks the accuracy marks â€” a bare '$3x^2+1$' or '$\frac{x+2}{3}$' with no method risks the M-marks. ISW means that once you have written the correct $f^{-1}(x)=\frac{x+2}{3}$, an unnecessary extra line does not cost you. And any correctly-rounded or algebraically-equivalent form of each answer is accepted.

**Bottom line:** of the 5 marks, three are method marks that survive an arithmetic slip, and the accuracy marks are shielded by follow-through â€” provided the substitution and the swap-and-solve are actually written down.

sections.content: The Practice button marks YOUR working exactly this way â€” analytically, mark by mark, with follow-through and equivalent forms accepted. So always show the method: for a composite, write the substitution $f(g(x))$ before simplifying; for an inverse, write $y=f(x)$, show the swap, then the rearrangement. Those method marks and the follow-through are what protect your score when a final line slips, and you only get that protection if the working is on the page.

Functions are the backbone of the rest of the course. The domain-and-range skills reappear the moment you meet rational, exponential and logarithmic functions, each with its own restrictions and asymptotes. Composition underlies the chain rule in calculus, where differentiating $f(g(x))$ is exactly unpicking a composite. Inverses return with logarithms as the inverse of exponentials and with inverse trigonometric functions, and the reflection in $y=x$ is the graphical fingerprint of every one of them. Master the habit â€” classify the function, state the domain and range in set notation, respect the order of composition, and check one-to-one before inverting â€” and the functions work throughout the course becomes variations on a method you already own.
